speaker: interviewer
text: Two of the Survivors of the Shoah, interview with Moshe Talbot. Did you experience any antisemitism as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. We were always afraid of the so-called Agaria pact. Pact. Pacts. Agaria pacts. Agaria were the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] teenagers-- Polish teenagers-- who just went out into the Jewish quarter of Cracow to beat up Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were always afraid of them. As a child, as a young boy of eight, nine, 10, 11, we always tried to avoid them. When we saw it from afar, when we saw them coming, we sprinted away. But sometimes it was too late. So many times I was beat up by those-- by those pacts, by the teenagers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, older people, adults also experienced antisemitism, no question about it. They had talked about it. They talked about-- about antisemitism at social gatherings, at family gatherings. Because what they experienced, perhaps myself as a young child did not experience because we were in a, as I-- as I told you before, I was in an all-Jewish school with all Jewish children. And all my friends and classmates and outside even school were all Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you think people were antisemitism when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: We felt-- and not only we felt, but the adults also told us-- that antisemitism was in-born. It was in-born. It was a part of national, religious life of the Poles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The fact that we as Jews were a part of Poland, lived in Poland, was ascribed to a-- an event, a very important event in history of the 15th century, I think, when the great King Kazimierz-- the great King Kazimierz of Poland invited the Jews to come and settle in Poland, because they were, at that time, I think they were banished from Poland. He invited them back, and since that time, Jews lived in Poland. But not all the kings and prime ministers and presidents were Kazimierz. After Kazimierz came different kings, different prime ministers and presidents who were not friendly to Jews. As a matter of fact, the last Prime Minister of Poland, his name was Smigly-Rydz, the last, before-- during the outbreak of the war, when the war broke out. He was a known antisemite. He didn't make any secrets out of it. He was an antisemite.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot remember of one. As a child, I cannot remember if I had a non-Jewish friend. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the neighbors? Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Neighbors were all Jews. We lived in a-- in a-- a neighborhood where they were all Jewish nei-- all Jewish people, in all the houses, in all the-- the entire neighborhood was Jewish. And so I did not even have an occasion or opportunity to meet and befriend a non-Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever travel with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times I did travel with him to Pzemysl, where he was born and was-- his family was there. My father took time off of his busy schedule twice or three times a year to go to Pzemysl to reunite with his family. His entire family was in Pzemysl, five brothers and three sisters. One sister-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not exactly. One sister lived in Tarnow, which was not far from Cracow, and one brother lived in Lemberg, which is not far from Pzemysl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lemberg. The oldest brother, who was a Hasidic Jew-- Hasidic Jew-- his name was Mordechai-- [INAUDIBLE]-- my father's oldest brother. And his sister who lived in Tarnow, her name was Chana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have a married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chana Schlesinger. Yes. Hannah Schlesinger. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And her son is now living in Ramat Gan in Israel. The only son that remained alive from the whole family in Tarnow lives in Ramat Gan.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Yakov Schlesinger. As a matter of fact, I-- just a few weeks ago, I was there to-- to visit them and spend some time with them. It was a very enjoyable time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know your grandparents at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I knew my grandparents of-- from my father side better than I knew the grandparents from my mother's side, because my grandfather on my mother side-- his name was-- his name was Pinchas Silbiger-- Silbiger, S-I-L-B-I-G-E-R, Silbiger. He died when I was five or six years old. So I faintly remember him. I remember him as being a very orthodox, very Hasidic Jew with a white beard. This is all I remember. And I remember when he was on his deathbed he called me over, and he squeezed me very lovingly. This is all I remember from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife's name, I think, was Rose. I'm not sure. She was also very devout-- a devout and very devoted Yiddishe mama. Jewish mama. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My other grandparents-- oh, this was a royal house. Really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was a kingly-- a-- a-- a king and a queen of the family. My fa-- my grandfather-- my grandfather whom you-- I have the picture-- his name was Feivel Taube-- Feivel Taube-- and my grandmother was Golda Taube. And they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were overseeing a house of great riches, I would say. A house of tradition, a house of devotion, and it was a royal house, as I said before because the-- the-- the deportment, the-- the-- the makings, the ingredients of all the-- of the family were so unforgettable. So unforgettable. When I came there, it was like coming to another world, to a world of-- of a fairy tale. For me it was a fairy tale, to meet all my cousins there, and all my aunts and uncles, and of course my grandfather. And he took me on his-- always on his knees and cuddled me and loved me so, so deeply. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did you know about the Germans before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the Germans before the war, I knew only that when I heard Hitler speak over the radio in 1937-- '35, '36, '37, up until the war, because the Polish radio broadcast his speeches to warn and to put on-- to put on attention the Polish people to the great dangers coming from this fiery oratory of Hitler. I know his speeches were heard in our house, because my father spoke German perfectly. He studied German, you see. He spoke German perfectly, and I know that he was extremely upset after every speech of Hitler. And this is where I was first exposed to the venom of Hitler, and to the threats of Hitler whom-- whom nobody believed. Nobody believed that he will ever have the opportunity to implement his threats and his ven-- and his-- his-- his hatred toward the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would your father tell you about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so petrified that he even didn't discuss It. He was just trans-- transfixed by the oratory. And, you know, Hitler knew how to-- how to-- how to spell-- spellbind the people. He knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever think of leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, unfortunately, no. Unfortunately. To our great misfortune. The business, the factory, it was thriving and-- and developing in the most beautiful manner. It blinded us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not only blinded us, but it was inconceivable. It was improbable to us that such misfortune, that such tragedy should strike so soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody, you see, it was a-- a mass-- a mass ignorance, or the will to ignore. Nobody, nobody stirred. Nobody stirred. There were very few cases where people left Poland to go to Eretz Yisrael. [INAUDIBLE] was my-- one of my many, many, many cousins who left to Eretz Yisrael.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was really-- Chana from Tarnow. You know? I just spoke to you about the sister in Tarnow? Her daughter with her newly wed husband, newly acquired husband, left to Eretz Yisrael right after they got married in 1937, I think it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Shoshana. Shoshana. Raizel. Shoshana, and his name was Jona Schneider. They decided to leave, but they were looked upon with pity. Look what they are doing. They are Zionists, they are leaving, and here we have such a beautiful life. Such a fine Jewish life. Such a juicy Jewish life, and they are leaving to go to Eretz Yisrael and to-- to start building there in terrible circumstances and conditions that are-- that are strange to us. Unknown. They are going into the unknown. Pity. And I wish-- I wish that I would be a part of them at that time, that we would have been a part of them-- my family would have been a part of them and had left. Then, our family would be safe, perhaps, with God's help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you were-- where you were when you heard about the war breaking out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the summer of 1939, the situation became so unbearable, so fraught with danger, that my father sent us away. My-- he sent me, my sister, and my mother away to Pzemysl to join the family there-- his family there, which Pzemysl was about 200 miles east towards the Soviet Union at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So perhaps if the Germans invade or something, perhaps they will-- they will not invade the whole Pol-- they will not conquer all of Poland. So at least we will be, perhaps, safe in Pzemysl. Safer in Pzemysl. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And sure enough, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] come Octo-- came October '39, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my cousin came running. The war had started. The Germans have invaded Poland. The war is starting. And my father, of course, right away joined us in Pzemysl. He left Cracow. He left the factory and everything else. He closed up and he came to Pzemysl. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And sure enough, the Germans were not far from Pzemysl, so we moved to Lemberg. We moved to his old-- older brother, to join his older brother. And we moved farther east to Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: By car, by bus, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by horse and buggy. Whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard to move around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard, because there were so many refugees. So many people who were fleeing from west to east Poland. Yes, and in Lemberg, in Novem-- October, yes? The whole-- the whole campaign, Polish campaign took two weeks, no more. Right?

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the end of two weeks, the Russians came and took over Lemberg from-- from the east and they-- they met on the San River with the-- with the Germans. And of course, this was all prearranged. And the famous Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact was all prearranged. So we were stuck-- we were stuck in Lemberg under the Russian occupation-- communist occupation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, I had to go to a school where they taught us to be good communists, because the Russians immediately took over everything-- schools, government, everything. Everything. And I had to go to school to be indoctrinated. I remember, I didn't want to write on Shabbas. I did not want to write on Shabbat. So they threatened. Threatened me, they threatened my father. What? What? What? Religion has nothing to do here with-- with education.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to be like everybody, like all the good Communists. Atheists. Nobody had the right or nobody even dared to bring-- to bring up religion or observance. No such thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I managed to somehow wiggle out of-- of desecrating Shabbat. To wiggle out in a-- in a clandestine manner.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just made myself like writing, but I really didn't. And there were teachers who were sympathetic, perhaps, who did not want to make an issue out of it. So they turned away-- turned away a blind eye. [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1939 was a year of upheaval in my life, and in the life of the whole family, and in the life of all Jews, because the beautiful house of cards that was built, the glorious house of cards that was built by the Jews in Poland, came tumbling down in 1939. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And many Jews realized what a fateful blunder it was to remain in Poland while they heard this propaganda, while they heard the threats coming out from Berlin. They realized, but it was too late, already. It was too late. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1939, my-- Could you still go to Shul under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no Shul. There were private houses where services were held. And of course, there was always a so-called sentry outside to see whether a Russian-- a Russian detachment isn't coming or something. There was no-- there was no possibility. The Shuls were closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that work out?

speaker: interviewee
text: It worked out. People went to private houses. They davened. They-- it was the same thing with the Germans. There were no Shuls. The Germans forbade all public worship by the Jews. [INAUDIBLE] they closed up the Shuls, and we davened in small-- in small apartments and people's private rooms.

speaker: interviewee
text: My bar mitzvah was in a private room in 1941? '40. 1940. In a private room, very quickly. Very quickly, because everybody was afraid the Germans might come and catch us davening. And they did. The did come, during my bar mitzvah. When I said half Torah, they came. Three or four Germans came up and said, what is this here? It's just a little thanksgiving to God. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of them asked, will He help you? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I turned to my mother, and my mother said, yes, He will. Of course, without the German hearing it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, in 1940, the Germans opened their side-- and the Russians-- opened their new border. Whoever wanted to go back to Cracow could go back to Cracow. Whoever wanted to remain on the Russian side, could remain on the Russians side. My father asked my mother and my sister to go back to Cracow, and see whether something of the factory can be salvaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come he didn't go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was afraid to go to the-- with the Germans. I mean, he was afraid to go with-- to be under the German occupation. So he and I remained there. But he-- after a few months, he sent me back to join my mother. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in May 1940. He remained in Crac-- in-- in Pzemysl at that time. Yes. No, in Lemberg. Psemysi was under the Russian occupation. He remained in Lemberg. He remained in Lemberg until 1941, when the Russ-- when the Germans invaded Russia in June, '41. Then they immediately conquered Lemberg, and he came back to Cracow and joined us in to ghetto of Cracow, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was it when you went back to Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean in 1940? When I went back to Cracow, first of all I had to put on, since I was already a bar mitzvah age, 13, I had to put on a band with a-- with a Magen David, Star of David, that indicated my faith or my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] Cain's spot, blot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The sign of Cain-- that I was Jew-- that I was Jewish. As such, I couldn't ride in any trolley. I couldn't take any buses. Nothing. Nothing. There were severe limitations, but it was livable. 1940 was livable, with all the limitations, with all the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hatred, still, which was in the-- in the very beginning. It was still livable in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go back to your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but our apartment was already shared with two other families [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who-- whose-- whose apartments were taken over by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just can't remember. Elderly people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So this was June, '41-- '40 was the time of my bar mitzvah. And I was tutored by a Hasidic Jew how to put on [INAUDIBLE], how to say my half Torah, how to be able to study Talmud. And he tutored me in different-- in different fields of Judaism, like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] customs, ceremonies, the reasons for certain things that we are doing, the reasons for things that we are forbidden to do, and the observance of Shabbat and so on and so on. This was a great-- for me, it was a great educational experience to study with such a man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot his name. I forgot his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Hasidic?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Hasidic Jew, but very intellectual. Extremely intellectual. I remember that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had a great sense of humor, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I loved him very much. I loved him very much. As I said before, the bar mitzvah was in a private room, in a private house where the service [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was held. And my father was, of course not-- not present because he was in Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at all. I think we-- after a few weeks, we received a card-- I received a card from him, a congratulatory card from Lemberg. Somehow, he smuggled it. I don't know how, but I remember receiving it from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for you, him not being there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you imagine? I mean, I was the only son, and I was his pride. And not be-- for him, not being there was very tragic. Very tragic. He told me this later on. But in-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were you doing at the time in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was studying. I was studying. I-- I-- We still had hope for some future. So my mother hired somebody, a teacher to study with me, material that I would usually study while in a gymnasium-- gymnasium means high school in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: The business was taken over by the Germans. The whole-- the entire factory was taken over by the Germans. There-- there was a German [GERMAN]. Means a-- a-- somebody who was entrusted, the trustee, by the German government. He took over the factory, and it was his.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that person from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was German. It was a German. As a matter of fact, it was two Germans. One deputy of his, and-- and he himself was a Berliner, actually, from Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot his name. I forgot. I can't remember his name. I only know that he tolerated my mother and me working-- working for him in our factory. Yes.
